en.extract:
The white-breasted wood wren (Henicorhina leucosticta) is a small songbird of the wren family. It is a resident breeding species from central Mexico to northeastern Peru and Suriname.


Description
The adult white-breasted wood wren is 10 centimetres (3.9 in) long and weighs 16 grams (0.56 oz). It has chestnut brown upperparts with a darker crown, pale supercilia, and black-and-white streaked sides of the head and neck. The underparts are white becoming buff on the lower belly. The wings and very short tail are barred with black. Young birds have duller upperparts and grey underparts.


Call

The call of this species is a sharp cheek or explosive tuck, and the song is cheer oweet oweet cheery weather; ornithologist and bioacoustics expert Luis Baptista of the California Academy of Sciences compared it to the opening bars of Beethoven's Fifth Symphony.
As with some other wrens, pairs often sing in duets.


Habitat
H. leucosticta breeds in lowlands and foothills up to 1,850 metres (6,070 ft) above sea level in tropical wet forest and adjacent tall second growth. Its neat roofed nest is constructed on the ground or occasionally very low in undergrowth, and is concealed by dense vegetation. The eggs are incubated by the female alone for about two weeks to hatching, and the young fledge in about the same length of time again. This species may build a "dormitory nest" for individuals or family groups, which is typically higher, than the breeding nest, up to 3 metres (9.8 ft) off the ground.
The white-breasted wood wren forages actively in low vegetation or on the ground in pairs in family groups. It mainly eats insects and other invertebrates


References

General references:


Further reading

The '''white-breasted wood wren''' ('''''Henicorhina leucosticta''''') is a small [[songbird]] of the [[wren]] family. It is a resident breeding species from central Mexico to northeastern Peru and [[Suriname]].

==Description==
The adult white-breasted wood wren is {{convert|10|cm|in}} long and weighs {{convert|16|g|oz}}. It has chestnut brown upperparts with a darker crown, pale [[supercilium|supercilia]], and black-and-white streaked sides of the head and neck. The underparts are white becoming buff on the lower belly. The wings and very short tail are barred with black. Young birds have duller upperparts and grey underparts.

==Call==
[[File:Beethoven symphony 5 opening.svg|right|frame|The opening bars of [[Symphony No. 5 (Beethoven)|Symphony No. 5]] by [[Ludwig van Beethoven]] {{audio|Beethoven symphony 5 opening.mid|Play}}, reminiscent of the white-breasted wood wren's song.]]
The [[Bird vocalization|call]] of this species is a sharp ''cheek'' or explosive ''tuck'', and the song is ''cheer {{not a typo|oweet oweet}} cheery weather''; [[ornithology|ornithologist]] and [[bioacoustics]] expert [[Luis Baptista]] of the [[California Academy of Sciences]] compared it to the opening bars of [[Symphony No. 5 (Beethoven)|Beethoven's Fifth Symphony]].<ref>{{cite episode| transcript-url=http://www.loe.org/shows/shows.htm?programID=00-P13-00030#feature4 | series=[[Living on Earth]]| transcript=Birds and Beethoven |air-date= July 28, 2000| network=[[National Public Radio]] <!-- the show later moved to [[Public Radio International]]-->| credits=Lauren Gravitz| quote=In the rainforest of [[Chiapas]], Mexico, [Baptista] heard the opening bars of Beethoven's Fifth Symphony coming from a white-breasted wood wren.}}</ref>

As with some other wrens, pairs often sing in [[Duetting|duets]].<ref>Meyer de Schauensee, Rodolphe & William H. Phelps (1978) ''A Guide to the Birds of Venezuela'', Princeton University Press.</ref>

==Habitat==
''H. leucosticta'' breeds in [[lowland]]s and [[foothills]] up to {{convert|1850|m|ft}} [[Meters above sea level|above sea level]] in [[tropical wet forest]] and adjacent tall [[secondary forest|second growth]]. Its neat roofed nest is constructed on the ground or occasionally very low in [[undergrowth]], and is concealed by dense vegetation. The eggs are incubated by the female alone for about two weeks to hatching, and the young [[fledge]] in about the same length of time again. This species may build a "[[dormitory nest]]" for individuals or family groups, which is typically higher, than the breeding nest, up to {{convert|3|m|ft}} off the ground.

The white-breasted wood wren forages actively in low vegetation or on the ground in pairs in family groups. It mainly eats [[insect]]s and other [[invertebrate]]s
